TR_Gasventing+50+Batteries__TR_Gasventin.prediction: Separator melting/shrinkage at 135–165°C can eliminate physical isolation between anode and cathode, causing internal short circuits. The short produces rapid Joule (I²R) heating and local hotspots, which accelerates the cell temperature rise and triggers additional reactions even before electrolyte decomposition dominates. This acceleration is distinct from the later 200°C+ stage because it is driven primarily by electrical short-circuit heating (and associated localized damage), not by bulk high-temperature chemical exothermic decomposition of the electrolyte.
TR_Gasventing+50+Batteries__TR_Gasventin.gold_answer: When the separator melts at 135-165°C, electrical contact between anode and cathode can occur, resulting in an internal short circuit and self-discharge. This self-discharge generates resistive heating (I²R losses) as current flows through the short circuit path, accelerating temperature rise before the major exothermic decomposition reactions at 200°C become dominant. This mechanism is distinct because it is electrochemical energy release (stored electrical energy converted to heat via short circuit) rather than chemical decomposition energy release (bond breaking and oxidation reactions). The separator melting enables a new heat generation pathway that is independent of the temperature-dependent reaction kinetics governing electrolyte and cathode decomposition.
TR_Gasventing+50+Batteries__TR_Gasventin.reason: Matches all key points: separator melting leads to loss of isolation and internal short, causing self-discharge/current flow with I^2R (Joule) heating that accelerates temperature rise before 200°C reactions. Clearly distinguishes this electrochemical/electrical short-circuit heating from later chemical exothermic decomposition.
